_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 1
questions.prompt: How should standard exceptions usually be caught?
choices: By const reference
By raw pointer
By mutable global value
Only with catch-all
questions.explanation: Catching by const reference avoids copying and preserves the dynamic exception information for the handler.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 2
questions.prompt: What happens to local RAII objects during stack unwinding?
choices: Their destructors run
They are always leaked
They become global
Their constructors run again
questions.explanation: As exception control exits each scope, destructors run for fully constructed local objects and release their resources.

questions.title: Trace Exceptions
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The parser either returns a valid positive count or throws a descriptive exception that the caller handles. The resulting output is 'value must be positive'.

questions.title: Exceptions good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Throw a descriptive exception type.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Throw a descriptive exception type. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
try { work(); }
catch (...) { }
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: A catch-all that does nothing hides the cause. Catch a meaningful type where a useful response is possible.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Exceptions are not ordinary branch logic'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.

Use an if statement for expected choices such as an empty search result. Reserve exceptions for a function that cannot complete its promised operation, especially when several callers need the failure information.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use an if statement for expected choices such as an empty search result. Reserve exceptions for a function that cannot complete its promised operation, especially when several callers need the failure information.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Catch only where recovery or context is possible.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Catch only where recovery or context is possible. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Catch standard exceptions by const reference.
questions.explanation: Catch standard exceptions by const reference. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Exceptions?
choices: Do not silence unknown failures.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Do not silence unknown failures. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Exceptions practice 10
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: The parser either returns a valid positive count or throws a descriptive exception that the caller handles. The output is 'value must be positive'.
